content.text:
[Thu 2026-04-09 14:57 GMT+2] [Subagent Context] You are running as a subagent (depth 1/1). Results auto-announce to your requester; do not busy-poll for status.

[Subagent Task]: You are NavaSM — a sharp social media copywriter for Nava Healthcare.

## Your task
Draft an X (Twitter) version of Post 3 — the continuity/merry-go-round post that just went live on LinkedIn.

## The LinkedIn post (for reference):
A travelling nurse reaches week seven of their contract and finally understands the culture and where the best karaoke in town is. Week nine, they've started anticipating which physicians prefer which workflows.

Then week thirteen arrives, and they leave.

This happens like clockwork, but not because nurses want to leave. Some negotiate to come back. Some choose their next contract carefully. But many leave because this is how to make a living in this industry.

The 13-week reset is a structural feature of the travel staffing model. Agencies profit on volume: new placements, new contracts, new orientation gaps. A nurse who knows your unit intimately is a nurse who's harder to place. Familiarity breeds comfort, and places unknown come with uncertainty and social anxiety. So the system moves them before they feel at home.

Your unit pays the price: hand-off errors, orientation costs, the institutional knowledge that never accumulates because it keeps walking out the door.

We built Nava differently. Continuity requires someone who stays, so we structure around that from day one:
 • Truth at placement,
 • Follow-up after arrival,
 • Relationships that outlast the contract cycle.

Because we don't deal in 13-week merry-go-rounds.

If you're tired of funding the learning curve, let's talk.

## Context
- WHO AM I riddle (Clue 2): "My experts are brought in at a premium to cover problems your organisation should be able to handle, then disappear a few months later."
- Post 3 is the answer to that riddle — it explains WHY they disappear
- X format: 280 chars per tweet, can thread up to 3-4 tweets
- X readers don't know Nava — stand alone
- Thread header: no emoji signals — design it to make readers want to keep reading

## X-specific rules
- Single tweets: compressed, punchy, no fluff
- Thread: each tweet is a chapter, not a standalone
- First tweet: scroll-stopper + navigation cue
- Contrast creates discovery — open with what readers think they understand, reveal what they don't
- Final tweet: data + diagnosis + urgency + CTA if earlier tweets built correctly
- "Time is ticking" closer addition — adds urgency without repeating argument structure
- Bold CAN appear in X threads for emphasis

## Editorial rules
- Gender neutral ("they" for individuals)
- No lore assumptions — X readers know nothing about Nava
- No "structure requires it" — humanize the mechanism
- End with a word that lands — merry-go-rounds, learning curve, etc.

## Output
Provide either:
1. A single tweet (≤280 chars) — if the full idea fits
2. A 2-3 tweet thread — if condensation requires it

Start directly with the first sentence — no preamble.</task>
<parameter name="runtime">subagent

**Tweet 1** (scroll-stopper + navigation)

They arrive knowing nothing. By week seven, they know your unit. Week thirteen, they're gone.
The 13-week exit isn't random. Here's the mechanism.

**Tweet 2** (mechanism revelation)

Agencies profit on **volume** — new placements, new contracts, new orientation gaps.
A nurse who knows your unit intimately is harder to place. Familiarity is bad for business.
So they reset before comfort sets in.

**Tweet 3** (impact + contrast + urgency)

Your unit absorbs: hand-off errors, orientation costs, and knowledge that keeps walking out the door.
We built Nava differently. Follow-up that outlasts placement. Relationships that outlast the contract.
Time is ticking — funding the learning curve has a cost.
